alerts.name: SQL Injection
alerts.desc: SQL injection may be possible. The page results were successfully manipulated using the boolean conditions.
alerts.solution: Do not trust client side input, even if there is client side validation in place. Use parameterised queries or prepared statements.

alerts.name: Cross Site Scripting (Reflected)
alerts.desc: Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is an attack technique that involves echoing attacker-supplied code into a user's browser.
alerts.solution: Validate all user input and encode all output. Use a Content Security Policy header.

alerts.name: Path Traversal
alerts.desc: The Path Traversal attack technique allows an attacker access to files, directories, and commands that potentially reside outside the web document root directory.
alerts.solution: Assume all input is malicious. Use an acceptance list of allowed file paths rather than denying specific paths.

alerts.name: Application Error Disclosure
alerts.desc: The response appears to contain an application error message. This can reveal sensitive information about the application's internals including SQL query details.
alerts.solution: Review the application's error handling. Do not expose sensitive information in error responses.

alerts.name: Absence of Anti-CSRF Tokens
alerts.desc: No Anti-CSRF tokens were found in a HTML submission form. A cross-site request forgery attack may be possible.
alerts.solution: Phase: Architecture and Design. Use a vetted library or framework that does not allow this weakness. Use CSRF token pattern.

alerts.alert: Web Browser XSS Protection Not Enabled
alerts.name: Web Browser XSS Protection Not Enabled
alerts.desc: Web Browser XSS Protection is not enabled or is disabled by the configuration of the 'X-XSS-Protection' HTTP response header.
alerts.solution: Ensure that the web browser's XSS filter is enabled by setting the X-XSS-Protection HTTP response header to '1'.

alerts.name: X-Frame-Options Header Not Set
alerts.desc: X-Frame-Options header is not included in the HTTP response to protect against 'ClickJacking' attacks.
alerts.solution: Most modern Web browsers support the X-Frame-Options HTTP header. Ensure it's set on all web pages returned by your site.

alerts.name: Strict-Transport-Security Header Not Set
alerts.desc: HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) is a web security policy mechanism. The server does not send an HSTS header.
alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to enforce Strict-Transport-Security.

alerts.name: X-Content-Type-Options Header Missing
alerts.desc: The Anti-MIME-Sniffing header X-Content-Type-Options was not set to 'nosniff'.
alerts.solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Content-Type header appropriately. Set the X-Content-Type-Options header to 'nosniff'.
